{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks whether repeatedly fluctuating PSA levels are reassuring enough to avoid biopsy. Reviewing the documents, particularly those related to active surveillance and PSA interpretation, reveals that stable PSA levels are generally reassuring and may support avoiding biopsy in low-risk cases. However, fluctuating levels are not explicitly described as reassuring. Document [0] states that two separate abnormal PSA levels 8 weeks apart are required to confirm an elevated PSA, and that 25% to 40% of men with an initial elevated PSA will have normal levels on recheck, suggesting that fluctuation may be transient and benign. However, fluctuating levels, especially with increases (e.g., >0.75 ng/mL or >25% over a year), are considered concerning and may indicate cancer. Document [14] lists a PSA increase of >0.75 ng/mL or >25% over one year as an indication for biopsy. Document [3] notes that significant changes in PSA (e.g., >0.75 ng/mL or >25% increase) prompt a rebiopsy. Therefore, while stable PSA levels may be reassuring, fluctuating levels—especially rising ones—are not considered reassuring and are associated with increased risk. Thus, fluctuating levels do not provide sufficient reassurance to avoid biopsy. The evidence supports that such fluctuations require further evaluation, not avoidance of biopsy.", "answer_choice": "B"}
